---
title: "Ingesting four decades of population-PK literature: challenges and a token-efficient, quality-preserving pipeline"
author: "nlmixr2 project"
date: "Draft — started 2026-06-22"
output: rmarkdown::html_document
---

<!--
This article is the living methods-paper draft for nlmixr2libingest. It is
deliberately structured like a journal manuscript so it can grow into one. At
this stage it is a set of notes; Results fill in as the pipeline is built and
benchmarked. The engineering counterpart is inst/design/ARCHITECTURE.md.
-->

## Abstract

Reproducing a published population pharmacokinetic / pharmacodynamic (popPK/PD)
model in executable form is a deceptively hard information-extraction problem.
The source corpus spans roughly four decades, multiple modelling tools and
notational traditions, and no enforced reporting standard. We describe
`nlmixr2libingest`, tooling that lets a large-language-model agent extract such
models into the `nlmixr2lib` library at scale while holding a strict quality
invariant: every estimated value and every nonstandard structural equation in
the final model is source-traced to the original paper. We frame the cost of
agentic extraction (dominated by cached-context re-reads across a build–check–fix
loop, not by the paper), catalogue the heterogeneity that makes extraction hard,
and present a deterministic-first architecture — log filtering, register lookup,
structural archetypes, and optional local-LLM distillation — whose every
acceleration is a cheaper *input* or a *prior*, never a gate on what the agent
reads of the paper.

## 1. Introduction

Population PK/PD models are published as a mix of prose, equations, tables, and
(sometimes) control streams. A reader who wants to *run* the model must recover
its compartmental structure, parameterisation, inter-individual and residual
variability, covariate relationships, and dosing/observation conventions, then
re-express them in a modelling language. Doing this faithfully for one paper is
routine expert work; doing it for thousands of papers is an industrial-scale
extraction problem with an unusual constraint — the output must be *numerically
faithful to a fixed ground truth* (the paper), so "plausible but wrong" is a
failure, not a minor error.

LLM agents can do this extraction, but at a cost structure that is unintuitive:
the paper is cheap (a trimmed paper is on the order of 8.6k tokens); the expense
is the **cached context re-read on every turn of the build–check–fix loop**,
multiplied by the number of turns. Reducing cost therefore means shrinking the
per-turn context, cutting the turn count, and not dispatching the agent at all on
the large fraction of indexed documents that are not fittable popPK papers.

The central design tension is that the cheapest way to shrink context — letting a
retrieval system or a small model decide what the agent sees — is exactly the way
to silently lose fidelity. Our resolution is a **quality firewall**: cheap
pre-processing may propose and prioritise, but the agent always verifies the
final numbers and nonstandard equations against the full paper, which is never
gated.

## 2. The heterogeneity problem (why this is hard)

Notes toward a taxonomy of what varies across the corpus. Each item is a concrete
extraction hazard, not just trivia.

- **No standardised reporting.** There is no enforced equivalent of CONSORT for
  popPK. Structural model, parameter table, variability model, and covariate
  equations may each be in text, a table, a figure, an appendix, or only in a
  supplementary control stream.
- **Tooling and notation drift over ~40 years.** NONMEM (ADVAN/TRANS macros,
  `THETA`/`ETA`/`EPS`), Monolix (mlxtran), ADAPT, WinNonlin, SAAM II, Phoenix,
  Stan, and hand-derived closed-form solutions all appear. The *same* model can
  be written as ODEs, as a closed-form `linCmt()`-style solution, or as ADVAN
  subroutine references the reader must expand.
- **Parameterisation ambiguity.** Clearance/volume vs micro-rate constants;
  `CL`/`V` vs `k`/`V`; absorption as first-order `ka`, transit chains, or
  zero-order; central volume reported as `V`, `V1`, `Vc`, or `V/F`. Apparent
  (`/F`) vs absolute parameters are frequently implicit.
- **Variability conventions.** IIV reported as variance, SD, CV%, or %; on log,
  logit, or additive scale; full vs block vs diagonal Ω; shrinkage sometimes
  conflated with estimates. Residual error as additive, proportional, combined,
  or log-normal, with units that must be inferred.
- **Units and scaling.** Mixed time units (h vs day), amount vs concentration,
  molar vs mass, allometric reference weights left unstated, BSA/FFM/IBW
  derivations (DuBois/Mosteller, Janmahasatian, Devine) often uncited.
- **Covariate model expression.** Centering references, fixed vs estimated
  exponents, indicator-variable encodings, and study-specific covariates whose
  meaning is not portable across papers (the motivation for a *scope* field in
  the covariate register).
- **Document quality.** Older papers are scanned images requiring OCR; tables
  break across pages; Greek letters and subscripts are mangled; errata and
  supplements live in separate files.
- **Non-fittable look-alikes.** GastroPlus/PBPK-QSP exports, posters, reviews,
  and methods papers are textually similar to fittable popPK papers but should
  not be dispatched for extraction.

## 3. Methods (the pipeline)

### 3.1 Cost model and quality firewall

We treat per-run cost as `cache_read × output`, where `cache_read` is roughly
(cached context size) × (number of agent turns). The optimisation targets are
therefore (a) per-turn context size, (b) turn count, and (c) the share of
dispatches spent on non-fittable papers. Every mechanism below is admissible
only under the firewall of Section 1: source-trace every final `ini()` value and
any nonstandard `model()` equation; never gate the paper; treat machine outputs
as priors.

### 3.2 Deterministic levers (no LLM required)

- **Log filtering.** `devtools::check()` and vignette-render logs are reduced
  deterministically to ERROR/WARNING/NOTE steps and failing chunks. Determinism
  is the point: an LLM summary that drops a real error would corrupt the loop.
- **Register lookup.** The three `nlmixr2lib` canonical-name registers
  (covariate ≈284k tokens, compartment, parameter) are parsed into a DuckDB
  full-text index; the agent retrieves the few relevant entries by name, alias,
  or concept instead of reading the file. The index rebuilds only when a source
  register changes.
- **Structural features and archetypes.** Each existing model is parsed (via its
  rxUi) into a structural feature vector — compartments, absorption, elimination,
  PD type, variability, covariates, residual error — augmented with its
  `description`/`reference`/`vignette` text. Clustering yields an empirical
  archetype taxonomy, with an explicit *other* bucket for unique-but-interesting
  structures that should be flagged rather than forced into a template.

### 3.3 Optional, pluggable LLM acceleration

When a local LLM is configured, embeddings (`nomic-embed-text`) drive a fittable
classifier and an archetype prior, and a small instruct model emits a structured
distillation sheet (parameters, values, units, RSE, IIV, covariate equations,
residual error, dosing) that the agent builds from and verifies against. With no
LLM, classification falls back to keyword/feature rules and distillation is
skipped — the agent reads the trimmed paper directly. The acceleration is never
load-bearing for correctness.

### 3.4 Scale orchestration

A pre-dispatch classifier annotates likely-non-fittable papers and sets an effort
tier (annotate-first; auto-skip only after held-out validation). A soft,
complexity-tiered budget checkpoints work and escalates to a human via a sidecar
question on overrun rather than hard-killing.

## 4. Results

Dollar figures use the runner's recorded blended rate (~$1.3/Mtok-total; cost is
dominated by cache-read). Token figures for re-runs were recovered from the
agents' own per-message usage transcripts.

### 4.1 Cost is cache-read × turns

Two original library extractions, from the runner's complete RunRecords:
vedolizumab 3.25M tokens (**2.86M cache-read**, 23k output), $4.27, ~20 turns;
the van Dijkman decline 1.79M tokens (1.54M cache-read), $2.67, 5 attempts.
**~96% of tokens — and of cost — are cache reads.** Empirically, run cost ≈ rate ×
Σ_turns(cached context), so the two control variables are *per-turn context size*
and *number of turns*.

### 4.2 Blind re-extraction accuracy

Re-extracting four already-curated papers from source alone, then cross-checking
against the shipped models and the papers: (i) a 1-cmt linear mAb — faithful,
11/11 values source-traced, and it actually **corrected a unit error in the
shipped model** (the paper reports ng/mL; the curated model used µg/mL, 1000× off
in simulation); (ii) a 2-cmt parallel-linear+Michaelis-Menten model with **13
covariates** — full structural and numeric match, 31/31 traced (incl. the
supplement's Table S4); (iii) a **PK-free algebraic exposure-response PD model** —
correct structure, but one real error (it used the residual *variance* 7.09 where
the *SD* 2.66 belongs); (iv) a meta-modelling catalogue of 11 cited models —
**correctly declined to fabricate**, matching the operator's original deselection.
Two value disagreements, one in each direction, both resolved by reading the
paper. The judgement-versus-mechanism boundary is real: the model structure and
most values extract reliably; unit conventions and variance-vs-SD encodings are
where errors hide.

### 4.3 A firewall limitation

The programmatic source-trace flags ini() values with no supporting number in the
paper, but it verifies *presence*, not *slot*: the erroneous residual `7.09` read
as "found" because that number *is* in the paper — as the variance. Catching a
right-number-wrong-role error still requires reading the labelled column. A
role-aware or LLM-cross-checked variant is future work.

### 4.4 The register-lookup lever (controlled A/B)

Same six-covariate task, two ways, with full transcript telemetry: reading
covariate-columns.md (~136k tokens) into context cost **17.6M tokens / $22.84**
(peak per-turn context 532k); resolving the same six via `lookup_canonical` cost
**0.47M tokens / $0.62** (peak context 44k) — **~37× cheaper, 12× smaller peak
context**, for identical answers. The lookup is a strict win *when an agent would
otherwise load the register.*

### 4.5 What actually dominates cost

Verbose, general-purpose re-extractions cost **5–14× the originals** ($41 vs $4.27
for the hard case) — but **not because of the register** (lookup-capable agents
never loaded it; their peak context stayed below the file size). The driver was
**turn count**: 126–267 model turns versus the tuned runner's ~20. Because cost ≈
context × turns, a ~10× turn inflation produced a ~10× cost inflation. The single
correctly-declined case (23 turns) was the only one that matched the original's
cost.

### 4.6 Supervised A/B on the live runner: the levers in a real extraction

Section 4.4 measured one lever in isolation; here the *combined* token-minimising
skill is measured end-to-end against the production skill on the real dispatch
runner, with full RunRecord telemetry (the accurate vehicle the earlier ad-hoc
probes lacked). Two skills were compared: the production `extract-literature-model`
and a `-lean` variant that (i) resolves every canonical covariate/parameter name
in one batched pre-brief instead of a lookup per name, and (ii) replaces the
separate vignette-render, convention-lint, and `R CMD check` gates with a single
`validate_model(level = "full")` call. Everything else was held fixed — same two
papers, same model (`claude-opus-4-7`), same effort, one task at a time on a
single account, byte-identical prompts except the skill name — and each cell was
run in triplicate.

*Aside (baseline provenance, for a later detailed write-up): the production skill
is `extract-literature-model` at nlmixr2lib commit `dc10fec8` (`origin/main` at
experiment time); the lean variant was committed at `2226ba8f`. Two of the twelve
runs were dropped as unreliable — one to an output-evidence-gate misconfiguration
(a complete extraction flagged "no output" because the task neither committed nor
declared a deliverable file), and one to a corrupted usage capture ($9.74 billed
against an implausible 102 output tokens).*

Every individual extraction run (the `$0` stop-sequence throttle-aborts that
precede some runs are omitted; `*` = excluded from the summary):

| paper | skill | rep | output | cache-read | total tok | cost | wall-clock |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| easy (1-cmt mAb) | production | 1 | 22,704 | 2,477,773 | 2,632,714 | $2.63 | 568 s |
| easy (1-cmt mAb) | production | 2 | 34,137 | 1,536,424 | 1,696,937 | $2.41 | 486 s |
| easy (1-cmt mAb) | production | 3 | 24,313 | 2,932,387 | 3,188,931 | $3.53 | 1015 s |
| easy (1-cmt mAb) | lean | 1\* | 27,126 | 4,714,280 | 4,888,691 | $3.96 | 1667 s |
| easy (1-cmt mAb) | lean | 2 | 22,578 | 1,388,453 | 1,505,667 | $1.85 | 745 s |
| easy (1-cmt mAb) | lean | 3 | 19,444 | 1,260,787 | 1,369,686 | $1.68 | 1116 s |
| hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) | production | 1 | 38,898 | 5,675,022 | 6,018,958 | $5.72 | 863 s |
| hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) | production | 2\* | 102 | 198,007 | 198,434 | $9.74 | 1439 s |
| hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) | production | 3 | 29,151 | 4,506,228 | 4,689,133 | $3.94 | 695 s |
| hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) | lean | 1 | 35,507 | 4,869,027 | 5,033,247 | $4.13 | 2179 s |
| hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) | lean | 2 | 34,360 | 6,404,502 | 6,570,678 | $4.89 | 1628 s |
| hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) | lean | 3 | 45,083 | 7,522,658 | 7,766,024 | $6.13 | 2569 s |

`*` easy/lean/1 is the output-gate-failed task; it ran a *second* complete
extraction too (run 4: $1.66 / 1.23M total / 1149 s) before being killed — both
excluded. hard/production/2 billed $9.74 against 102 output tokens (inconsistent
usage capture) — excluded.

Means over the included runs (cost | cache-read | wall-clock): easy production
$2.86 | 2.32M | 690 s vs lean **$1.77 | 1.32M** | 930 s; hard production $4.83 |
5.09M | 779 s vs lean $5.05 | 6.27M | 2125 s.

The headline is that **the token-minimising skill's benefit is
complexity-dependent, not universal.** On the easy model the lean variant was
~38 % cheaper (−43 % cache-read); on the hard model it was ~5 % *more* expensive
(+23 % cache-read) and ~2.7× slower in wall-clock. The batched pre-brief saves
turns when a paper has many covariates, but on the complex model that saving was
more than offset by the unified `validate_model(level = "full")` step — a full
`R CMD check` plus vignette render over the entire library — and by the lean
agent simply taking more turns. Run-to-run variance was large (the easy-model
production runs spanned $2.41–3.53; one banked lean run cost $3.96 against a
typical ~$1.7), which is why cells were replicated; even so n is small and the
hard-model figure should be read as "no clear win," not a precise penalty.

This is consistent with Section 4.5: the dominant lever is *turn count*, and an
input-side skill change helps most when the extraction is otherwise short. When a
model is complex, a validation step that is itself heavy can erase the input-side
savings — so the unified validator should be scoped to the new model rather than
rebuilding the whole package before being adopted as a default.

## 5. Discussion

The levers in this package are aimed correctly — at *per-turn context* (register
lookup, log filtering, a lean lazily-loaded skill) — and they save real money when
triggered (lookup: ~37× on an isolated task). But the **dominant** cost driver in
practice is the **number of turns**, which is a property of the dispatching
agent's discipline more than of the tooling. The practical conclusion is that the
biggest savings come from a tuned, low-turn dispatch (as the production runner
already is), with the input-side levers compounding on top.

Several further input-side pre-processing steps follow directly from the cost
model and are candidates for the low-turn path: a cheap local-LLM pass that reads
the paper, identifies its covariates, and emits a *focused, model-specific
covariate report* (paper name → canonical name/units/reference) so the agent
neither loads the register nor spends turns looking covariates up; a *single
code-based validation step* that runs parse, conventions, source-trace,
registry-build, check, and vignette-render and returns one terse "Success" or a
short list of file-and-line fixes, replacing the verbose logs that otherwise bloat
every subsequent turn; and a *policy-driven sidecar auto-responder* that answers
recurring stop-and-ask questions from a vetted policy table on the next tick, so a
run continues on a warm cache instead of paying a cold retry. Each keeps the
expensive judgement with the agent while moving deterministic or pre-decidable
work off the per-turn critical path.

Open limitations remain: the source-trace presence-vs-slot gap above; the
portability cost of a local-LLM dependency (mitigated by graceful degradation);
the reliance on small local models for distillation, which the source-trace
firewall is designed to catch; and the boundary discipline between this ingestion
package and the validated library it feeds, which must stay one-directional
(consume, never reimplement validation).

## 6. Conclusion

A token-efficient extraction pipeline need not trade away fidelity. By keeping
every acceleration on the *input* and *prior* side of a hard source-trace
firewall, and by making the LLM optional rather than central, the same
deterministic core that lowers cost also keeps the agent honest.
